title: Methods
text:
RNA-sequencing data were obtained from the publicly available dataset GSE135251, originally published in Transcriptomic signatures of fibrosis progression in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. The dataset includes liver biopsy samples spanning multiple stages of Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease progression. In the original study, fibrosis was categorized into five stages (F0–F4). For this analysis, fibrosis stages were consolidated into three biologically meaningful groups to improve statistical power and simplify interpretation: Control (F0): no fibrosis (n = 8) Early fibrosis (F1–F2): mild disease (n = 138) Moderate/advanced fibrosis (F3–F4): severe disease (n = 68) This increased the sample size within each category and allowed for a better detection of gene expression differences associated with fibrosis progression.



Raw RNA-seq count data were imported into R and processed using standard workflows. Sample metadata were matched to expression data to ensure correct alignment of fibrosis stage labels with sequencing samples.In addition to regrouping fibrosis stages into three categories (control, early, and moderate), we refined the dataset by excluding samples that lacked clear fibrosis stage annotations or had incomplete metadata, and we standardized gene identifiers to ensure compatibility with downstream enrichment tools. This step ensured consistency between expression data and clinical grouping while maintaining data quality.



Differential gene expression analysis was performed using DESeq2, a method specifically designed for count-based RNA-seq data. DESeq2 models count data using a negative binomial distribution and apply normalization to account for sequencing depth and dispersion. Comparisons were conducted between fibrosis groups to identify genes whose expression changes with disease progression. Genes with statistically significant adjusted p-values were considered differentially expressed.



To assess data structure and results, multiple visualization techniques were used: - Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to evaluate sample clustering by fibrosis stage - Volcano plots to display significance versus magnitude of gene expression changes - Heatmaps to visualize expression patterns of key genes across samples These visualizations supported interpretation of both global transcriptomic variation and pathway-specific trends.

title: Methods
text:
2 Pathway Enrichment Analysis
To interpret gene-level changes in a biological context, pathway enrichment analysis was performed using clusterProfiler. This tool identifies overrepresented biological pathways among differentially expressed genes. Analyses focused on pathways relevant to NAFLD progression, including extracellular matrix (ECM) organization, inflammation, lipid metabolism, and insulin resistance.

title: Conclusions
text: The study demonstrates the progression of early to moderate fibrosis in non alcoholic fatty livers is characterized by structural remodeling and fibrosis reinforcement pathways. A previous transcriptomic study by Wang et al. observed similar patterns of upregulation of the extracellular matrix pathways, resulting in hepatic stellate cell activation and excessive collagen deposition. Stellate cells activate the collagen production via ECM remodeling, changing the overall architecture of the liver (Wang 2022), which can be also supported by the findings of our enrichment pathway analyses. Our study also showed downregulation of metabolic and signaling pathways, suggesting a loss in normal liver cell functions, which can change the composition of the cells with the fibrosis advancements (Pantano 2021). A lot of studies done previously emphasize inflammation as the major, dominant factor of fibrosis progression, however, our findings indicate that ECM remodelling plays a much more significant role in the progression of fibrosis. When looking at past data and our results, it can be inferred that inflammation is a feature of the fibrosis disease, but structural reorganization is a more defining feature to distinguish the stages. Our study adds some new insight into fibrosis progression, showing that functional reprogramming of the liver can mark the stages more distinctly than signalling differences. By highlighting ECM driven remodelling as the defining molecular shift at these intermediate stages of the disease can help refine and distinguish NAFLD progression.

title: Conclusions
text: The study demonstrates the progression of early to moderate fibrosis in non alcoholic fatty livers is characterized by structural remodeling and fibrosis reinforcement pathways. A previous transcriptomic study by Wang et al. observed similar patterns of upregulation of the extracellular matrix pathways, resulting in hepatic stellate cell activation and excessive collagen deposition. Stellate cells activate the collagen production via ECM remodeling, changing the overall architecture of the liver (Wang 2022), which can be also supported by the findings of our enrichment pathway analyses. Our study also showed downregulation of metabolic and signaling pathways, suggesting a loss in normal liver cell functions, which can change the composition of the cells with the fibrosis advancements (Pantano 2021). A lot of studies done previously emphasize inflammation as the major, dominant factor of fibrosis progression, however, our findings indicate that ECM remodelling plays a much more significant role in the progression of fibrosis. When looking at past data and our results, it can be inferred that inflammation is a feature of the fibrosis disease, but structural reorganization is a more defining feature to distinguish the stages. Our study adds some new insight into fibrosis progression, showing that functional reprogramming of the liver can mark the stages more distinctly than signalling differences. By highlighting ECM driven remodelling as the defining molecular shift at these intermediate stages of the disease can help refine and distinguish NAFLD progression.

title: Conclusions
text:
2 Limitations
There are several limitations to be considered when interpreting the results of the data. First, even though the sample size is relatively large, the general grouping of fibrosis stages as early and moderate may have masked some subtle, specific changes within each specific fibrosis stage. Second, the metadata given in the original dataset did not specify the different cell types used in the samples, which can change how each different gene was expressed and how the changes were driven. There was also the possibility of misleading results from the pathway enrichments. As pathway enrichment relies on preexisting gene annotations, some of the annotated functions have no biological relevance to livers due to overlapping genes in databases. Even though some statistical thresholds were applied, the small effects that are biologically meaningful may have been overlooked due to these limitations.

title: Conclusions
text:
3 Future Directions
Future studies can build their analyses by following patients over a period of time, which could provide stronger evidence for gene relationships to fibrosis progression. More specific investigation of inflammation and insulin resistance pathways can also help with creating databases with more clarity and specificity in order to have a more successful and precise pathway enrichment analysis. The addition of understanding other functional changes in the liver can help find factors that relate to these changes. Understanding the differentially depressed genes can also help identify new therapies for fibrosis progression.

title: Non Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease & Fibrosis Transcriptomics
text: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is a condition in which excess fat accumulates in the liver and can progress to more severe stages such as inflammation, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Fibrosis is characterized by the excessive buildup of extracellular matrix (ECM) components, which disrupts the normal structure and function of liver tissue. As fibrosis worsens, these structural changes can significantly impair liver function. Because of this, understanding the molecular changes that occur during fibrosis progression is important for identifying potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets. In this study, we analyzed RNA-seq data from the GSE135251 dataset, which contains liver biopsy samples from 216 human patients. These samples were classified into three groups: control, early fibrosis, and moderate fibrosis. We hypothesized that as the fibrosis stage increases, the expression of genes involved in extracellular matrix organization would also increase. To test this, raw count data were processed using DESeq2 to identify differentially expressed genes between early and moderate fibrosis samples. Principal component analysis (PCA) was used to examine overall transcriptomic differences across all samples and to see how the groups clustered. The results of the differential expression analysis were visualized using a volcano plot, which highlights significantly upregulated and downregulated genes, and a heatmap showing the expression patterns of the most significant genes. In addition, Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis was performed using clusterProfiler to identify biological processes that were overrepresented among the differentially expressed genes. Our results showed clear transcriptomic differences between control and fibrosis samples, while differences between early and moderate fibrosis were present but less pronounced. GO enrichment analysis revealed that genes associated with extracellular matrix organization were significantly upregulated in moderate fibrosis. These findings support our hypothesis and suggest that fibrosis progression is associated with increased expression of ECM related genes, which may play a key role in disease severity.

title: Non Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease & Fibrosis Transcriptomics
text:
2 Introduction
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is a condition where excess fat builds up in the liver. It is one of the most common liver diseases worldwide and affects a large portion of the population (Younossi et al., 2016). NAFLD is closely linked to metabolic conditions such as obesity, insulin resistance, and systemic inflammation, making it an important part of cardiometabolic disease. As the disease progresses, it can lead to more serious conditions, including inflammation, fibrosis, and eventually cirrhosis (Friedman et al., 2018). Because of how common it is and how it connects to other health problems, NAFLD is considered a major public health concern.
NAFLD develops gradually and moves through different stages. It usually starts with simple fat accumulation in the liver, but over time it can progress to inflammation and fibrosis. Among these stages, fibrosis is especially important because it is strongly linked to disease severity and long-term outcomes (Angulo et al., 2015). Fibrosis refers to the buildup of scar tissue in the liver, which can interfere with normal liver function. This makes it important to understand how fibrosis develops and what biological processes are involved.
The development of NAFLD is not caused by a single factor. Instead, it is explained by the “multiple-hit” hypothesis, which suggests that several processes happen at the same time to drive disease progression (Buzzetti et al., 2016). These include fat accumulation, inflammation, oxidative stress, and other cellular changes. Inflammation plays a major role by damaging liver cells and activating pathways that lead to fibrosis (Tilg & Moschen, 2010; Koyama & Brenner, 2017). These inflammatory signals are connected to fibrosis through biological pathways that control how the liver responds to injury and repair (Seki & Schwabe, 2015). Over time, repeated injury and inflammation lead to long-term structural changes in the liver. One of the main features of fibrosis is the buildup of the extracellular matrix (ECM). The ECM is made up of proteins such as collagen that normally help support tissue structure. However, during chronic liver injury, this process becomes unbalanced and leads to too much ECM being produced (Wynn, 2008). Hepatic stellate cells, which are normally inactive, become activated during liver damage and start producing large amounts of ECM (Tsuchida & Friedman, 2017). This process is a key driver of fibrosis and has been widely described as the main mechanism behind scar tissue formation in the liver (Hernandez-Gea & Friedman, 2011). As ECM accumulates, it replaces healthy liver tissue and disrupts normal liver function (Bataller & Brenner, 2005). As fibrosis progresses, ECM continues to accumulate, making the liver stiffer and less able to function properly (Acharya et al., 2021).
In addition to structural changes, metabolic dysfunction also plays an important role in NAFLD. Insulin resistance is one of the key factors, as it causes the liver to store more fat and affects normal metabolic processes (Samuel & Shulman, 2012). Fat in the liver comes from several sources, including dietary intake, fat stored in adipose tissue, and fat produced within the liver itself (Donnelly et al., 2005). Because of these processes, NAFLD is strongly linked to conditions such as obesity and type 2 diabetes (Bugianesi et al., 2005; Tilg et al., 2017). These metabolic changes can make inflammation and fibrosis worse, creating a cycle that drives disease progression.
As NAFLD progresses, changes also occur at the molecular level. Gene expression patterns in the liver shift over time, with a decrease in genes involved in normal metabolism and an increase in genes related to inflammation and tissue remodeling (Arendt et al., 2015). These changes help explain how the disease develops and progresses at a deeper level. Studying these gene expression patterns can provide important insights into the biological mechanisms behind NAFLD.
Transcriptomic profiling provides a way to study these changes in detail. RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) is a powerful method that allows researchers to measure gene expression across the entire genome (Wang et al., 2009). This makes it possible to identify which genes are more or less active in different disease stages. Standard workflows help ensure that RNA-seq data is analyzed accurately and consistently (Conesa et al., 2016), while modern pipelines make it easier to identify genes that are differentially expressed between groups (Nagai et al., 2024). These tools allow researchers to connect changes in gene expression to biological processes such as extracellular matrix organization.
Publicly available datasets provide valuable opportunities to study NAFLD using real patient samples. The GSE135251 dataset includes liver biopsy samples across different fibrosis stages and has been widely used to study gene expression changes in NAFLD (Govaere et al., 2020). Previous studies using this dataset have shown that fibrosis progression is associated with increased expression of genes involved in extracellular matrix organization and tissue remodeling (Govaere et al., 2020). These findings suggest that ECM plays a key role in fibrosis progression and highlight the importance of studying these pathways.

title: Non Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease & Fibrosis Transcriptomics
text:
3 Project roadmap (living)

Public dataset: GEO GSE135251
Organism: Human
Tissue: Liver biopsy
Data type: RNA-seq raw counts
Sample size: 216 patients
Variables: Early (Fibrosis Stage: 1-2), Moderate(Fibrosis Stage: 3-4), Control(Fibrosis Stage: 0)


3.1 Method 1

Check and align metadata with count data
Remove low-count genes
Differential gene expression analysis using DESeq2
Compare early vs. moderate fibrosis
Identify significantly upregulated and downregulated genes



3.2 Method 2

Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis using clusterProfiler
Identify biological pathways (especially ECM-related pathways)
Optional: visualize pathway results using dot plots



3.3 Final figures + interpretation

PCA plot (sample clustering)
Volcano plot (significant gene changes)
Heatmap (top genes)
Bar plots (top upregulated/downregulated genes)
GO enrichment plots (pathways)
Link gene changes to fibrosis progression
Focus on ECM and tissue remodeling ### Limitations + ethics + reproducibility notes
Bulk RNA-seq includes mixed cell types
Patient variation may affect results
Grouping stages may hide smaller differences
Public dataset → no direct patient interaction (ethical use)
Code and analysis steps will be documented for reproducibility

title: Results
text:
Results were evaluated through multiple visualizations, including principal component analysis (PCA), volcano plots, and heatmaps, to identify patterns in gene expression across fibrosis stages. These figures were interpreted to assess differential expression trends and pathway-level changes, particularly in extracellular matrix (ECM) organization. Final outputs and interpretations were compiled and presented using Quarto for reproducible reporting.
 The PCA plot shows that PC1 accounts for 17.8% of the total variance and PC2 accounts for 8.4%, meaning that together they explain about 25% of the overall variation in gene expression across the samples. In this plot, proximity between samples indicates more similar gene expression profiles, while greater distance reflects larger differences. The clear separation between control and fibrotic samples along PC1 suggests that the transition from control to fibrosis represents the largest shift in gene expression. This major change is consistent with our hypothesis that genes related to extracellular matrix organization become increasingly expressed as fibrosis develops. In contrast, the early and moderate fibrosis groups cluster more closely together, indicating that their gene expression patterns are more similar. This suggests that once fibrosis is established, changes in gene expression occur more gradually. Overall, the PCA supports the idea that the most significant transcriptional changes happen during the initial onset of fibrosis, with more subtle progression afterward.
 The volcano plot displays differential gene expression, where the x-axis represents log2 fold change (direction of regulation) and the y-axis represents statistical significance. Genes shown in magenta are upregulated in advanced fibrosis, while blue genes are downregulated. The plot reveals that the top 10 most significant genes are all upregulated, indicating a strong bias toward increased gene expression in advanced fibrosis. Notably, genes such as LOXL4, FBLN5, and MMP7 are highly expressed and are known to play important roles in extracellular matrix organization, elastic fiber assembly, and tissue remodeling. The high position of these genes on the plot indicates that their upregulation is also statistically significant. This pattern suggests that extracellular matrix-related pathways are strongly activated in advanced fibrosis. Overall, the volcano plot supports our hypothesis by showing that genes involved in extracellular matrix organization are significantly upregulated as fibrosis progresses.
 The heat map visualizes gene expression patterns across samples, where red indicates high expression and blue indicates low expression. Clustering of samples reveals that early and moderate fibrosis groups have distinct gene expression profiles. Early fibrosis samples display more blue, suggesting lower expression levels of the selected genes. In contrast, moderate fibrosis samples show more red, indicating increased gene expression as the disease progresses. Notably, genes such as MMP7, FBLN5, and LOXL4 exhibit higher expression in moderate fibrosis and are known to be involved in extracellular matrix organization and fibrosis. This pattern highlights a shift toward increased activation of ECM-related pathways. Overall, the heat map supports our hypothesis by demonstrating that gene expression, particularly of ECM-related genes, increases with advancing fibrosis.
 The bar graph represents the top 50 downregulated genes in early and moderate fibrosis. The x-axis represents a log2 fold change, and the y-axis has the list of all the genes. All the negative fold change values show a decrease of expression of the genes as fibrosis progresses. When comparing the downregulated to the upregulated genes, the range of the fold changes is narrower, indicating a more consistent downregulation across all the genes. The downregulated genes are mainly associated with metabolic processes, signalling pathways, and normal liver functions. The pattern of the genes supports the data stating that the liver cells lose their normal functions as fibrosis progresses. Overall, the downregulated genes decrease metabolic functions of the liver, increasing the structures of fibrosis progression as seen in the upregulated genes.
 The bar graph shows the top 50 upregulated genes in the early and moderate fibrosis stages. The x-axis represents a log2 fold change, and the y-axis has the list of all the genes. There is an indication of increased fibrosis expression due to the positive changes in the log2 fold change values. Some genes show greater changes, suggesting a stronger upregulation of those specific functions. Many of these genes are associated with ECM organization, structural remodeling, and fibrosis progression. Most of the genes have similar fold changes, indicating a coordinated increase in fibrosis expression among the upregulated genes. Overall, this analysis supports the pattern of fibrosis progression via widespread activation of genes involved in remodeling and organization.
 The pathway dot plot displays the top 50 upregulated biological pathways, where color indicates statistical significance, with red showing more significant pathways and blue indicating less significant ones. Overall, the plot shows a relatively even distribution of gene involvement across multiple pathways, with a few pathways standing out more strongly than others. The most prominent and statistically significant pathway is epidermis development, indicating strong activation of genes involved in tissue growth, repair, and structural remodeling. Other enriched pathways, such as extracellular matrix organization, extracellular structure organization, and external encapsulating structure organization, further support the idea that tissue remodeling processes are highly active. Additionally, pathways related to muscle contraction and epithelial differentiation suggest broader changes in tissue function and structural integrity during fibrosis progression. The variation in circle sizes shows that some pathways involve more genes than others, but most remain consistently represented, suggesting coordinated biological activity rather than a single dominant process. Overall, this analysis indicates that upregulated genes in fibrosis are strongly associated with tissue development and extracellular matrix remodeling, supporting increased structural reorganization as fibrosis progresses.
 The downregulated pathway analysis indicates that several biological processes decrease in activity as fibrosis progresses in NAFLD. Notably, the most statistically significant pathways, shown in red, such as phototransduction and detection of light stimulus, suggest a reduction in sensory and signaling-related gene expression. Although these pathways are not traditionally central to liver function, their suppression may reflect broader disruptions in cellular communication and normal physiological activity within the liver. Additional pathways related to stimulus detection and digestion also show decreased expression, further supporting a decline in typical metabolic and signaling functions. This overall pattern suggests that as fibrosis advances, the liver shifts away from maintaining normal homeostatic processes. In contrast to these downregulated pathways, our hypothesis predicted that genes involved in extracellular matrix (ECM) organization would increase with fibrosis progression. Together, these findings support our hypothesis by showing that while normal liver functions are suppressed, resources may be redirected toward ECM remodeling and fibrotic tissue development.

# If using R, put code here (optional)
